frameworks.name: drift-diffusion / evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: behavioral modeling decomposes history bias into accumulation-bias vs starting-point components

frameworks.name: noradrenergic prior-reduction account
frameworks.one_line: catecholamines reduce the impact of priors on inference and decision

frameworks.name: choice history bias / dynamic priors
frameworks.one_line: tendency to repeat or alternate previous choices acts as an implicit prior

core_question: Do elevated catecholamine levels causally reduce choice history biases in perceptual decision-making?
key_assumptions: noradrenaline modulates the weighting of priors on perceptual inference
choice history bias reflects a dynamic prior carried across trials
drift-diffusion components (drift bias vs starting-point bias) can be separated behaviorally
manipulations: pharmacological elevation of catecholamines via atomoxetine vs placebo
within-subject perceptual decision task under uncertainty
key_findings: choice history biases were highly idiosyncratic, repetition for some and alternation for others
atomoxetine reduced history biases regardless of their direction
the bias reduction was captured by reduced drift bias in evidence accumulation
starting-point bias was not the locus of the catecholaminergic effect
interpretations: catecholamines causally diminish the impact of dynamic priors on perceptual decisions
noradrenergic effects on bias operate through evidence accumulation rather than baseline preference
